one-table-vs-multiple-tables.label: One table vs multiple tables
one-table-vs-multiple-tables.summary: Understand when to use one table or multiple tables in a relational database, and how this choice affects data organization and querying.

sketch1.title: Example: One Table vs. Multiple Tables

fillblank-1.title: Avoiding Repetition
fillblank-1.prompt: Which term describes the problem of storing the same information in many places within a single table?
fillblank-1.hint: It's a word for unnecessary repetition.

help.concept: the missing term means storing the same data in multiple places, which can lead to inconsistencies.
help.hint_1: This problem makes updates harder and can cause errors if not all copies are changed.
help.hint_2: The term starts with 'r' and is the opposite of efficiency in data storage.

fillblank-1.template: The problem of repeated information in one table is called ___.

fillblank-2.title: Purpose of Multiple Tables
fillblank-2.prompt: Why do databases often use more than one table?
fillblank-2.hint: Think about what makes data easier to manage.

help.concept: Multiple tables help structure information so each fact is stored only once and can be easily updated.
help.hint_1: This approach helps keep related data together and separate unrelated data.
help.hint_2: The word means 'arranged in a systematic way.'

fillblank-2.template: Databases use multiple tables to keep data ___ and avoid repetition.

singlechoice-1.title: Identifying the Best Table Design
singlechoice-1.prompt: Which scenario is best handled by using more than one table?
singlechoice-1.hint: Look for a situation where one thing can be linked to many others.

help.concept: When you have a many-to-many relationship, like students and classes, multiple tables prevent repeated data.
help.hint_1: If a student can take many classes, and each class has many students, one table isn't enough.
help.hint_2: The scenario with students and classes needs more than one table to avoid repeating student or class info.

options.a: A school wants to track students and the classes they take.
options.b: A list of colors and their hex codes.
options.c: A single grocery list for one person.
options.d: A table of country names and their capitals.

multichoice-1.title: When to Use Multiple Tables
multichoice-1.prompt: Which of the following are good reasons to use multiple tables in a database? (Choose all that apply.)

help.concept: Multiple tables help reduce redundancy, make updates easier, and keep data organized by topic.
help.hint_1: Storing unrelated data together is not a benefit.
help.hint_2: The correct reasons are about reducing repetition, making updates easier, and organizing by subject.

options.a: To avoid repeating the same information in many rows
options.b: To make updates easier and more accurate
options.d: To organize data by different subjects or entities

codeinput-1.title: Simple Query from a Single Table
codeinput-1.prompt: Suppose you have a table called `students_intro` with columns `id`, `name`, and `grade`. Write a SQL query to select all columns for students in grade 10.
codeinput-1.hint: Focus on the concept being tested.

help.concept: Think about the role or idea being tested rather than repeating answer wording.
help.hint_1: Eliminate choices or interpretations that do not match the task.
help.hint_2: Choose the concept that best fits what the exercise is asking you to do.

codeinput-1.starterCode: -- Write a SQL query to show all students in grade 10

sketch-1.title: Why Split Data Across Tables?
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown: In a relational database, you can store all your data in one big table, or you can split it into several smaller tables. Splitting data into multiple tables helps avoid repeating information, keeps data organized, and makes updates easier. For example, in a school database, you might have a `students` table and a `classes` table, instead of putting all student and class info in one place.

sketch-2.title: Example: One Table vs. Multiple Tables
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown: Suppose you want to track which students are in which classes. If you use one table, you might have columns for student name, class name, and teacher. But if a student takes multiple classes, their name and info get repeated. Instead, you can use a `students` table, a `classes` table, and a `student_classes` table to link them. This way, each piece of information is stored only once.
